metadata.file_name: 53. Tin et al. (2024). Educational Big Data Mining: Comparison of Multiple Machine Learning Algorithms in Predictive Modelling of Student Academic Performance.pdf
metadata.title: Educational Big Data Mining: Comparison of Multiple Machine Learning Algorithms in Predictive Modelling of Student Academic Performance
metadata.venue: International Journal of Advanced Computer Science and Applications (IJACSA)

survey_design.weight_fields_interpretation: The study uses PISA microdata covering students aged 15 years 3 months to 16 years 2 months from 79 countries, with exclusions at both school and student levels (e.g., excluding geographically inaccessible/special needs schools and students with intellectual/functional disabilities or limited language proficiency). The manuscript does not report applying PISA sampling weights or any replicate-weight procedure (e.g., BRR/jackknife) in the ML training/evaluation pipeline. Because the authors implement multiple classification algorithms in RapidMiner and describe a standard random 80/20 train-test split, the analysis appears to be conducted without complex-survey weighting. The paper also performs data cleaning (list deletion when missingness exceeds 50%, otherwise mode imputation) and feature selection/attribute selection prior to model training.

sample_details.sample_filtering_criteria: Used PISA eligible student population aged 15y3m–16y2m enrolled in grade 7 or higher, from 79 participating countries, with exclusions at school level (geographically inaccessible schools and special needs schools) and at student level (students with intellectual/functional disabilities and students with limited language proficiency in the PISA testing environment). The analytic dataset includes 612,004 respondents from both computer-based and paper-based tests. For data cleaning, variables with missingness >50% were removed via list deletion; otherwise missing values were imputed (mode for categorical variables in the provided example).

main_findings.dataset_used: PISA 2018 (ages 15y3m–16y2m), 79 countries — Reading/Math/Science/Global Competence PVs discretized to CGPA categories
main_findings.target_variable: Academic performance (AP) categories A–D (proxy for CGPA)
top_predictors: Reading skills and habits (e.g., ST013Q01TA, ST161Q06HA, ST154Q01HA)
main_findings.performance_metrics: Gradient Boosted Trees (GBT): Accuracy 73.7%, Precision 72.8%, Recall 88.3% (RapidMiner). Other models ranged roughly 65.7%–73.4% accuracy; Decision Tree had the lowest accuracy/recall.
main_findings.standardized_conclusion: Using PISA 2018 the study leveraged reading skills and habits, home learning environment, career goals/mindset, family background, and mental health to predict academic performance categories (A–D) derived from PISA plausible values, finding that Gradient Boosted Trees achieved the highest classification performance (Accuracy 73.7%, Recall 88.3%). The paper discretizes PV-based performance into four classes and reports train-test split performance, but it does not report complex-survey weighting or a clear PV-combination rule beyond PV-based discretization. This indicates that these patterns have implications for educational policy and instructional practice, though cross-sectional ILSA designs cannot establish causality.

data.outcome_summary: The study uses PISA 2018 microdata (612,004 students across 79 countries) and constructs an academic performance (AP) outcome by discretizing PISA plausible values into four categories (A–D), described as a proxy for CGPA. After cleaning missing values (list deletion when missingness >50%, otherwise imputation) and selecting attributes from five conceptual domains (demographics, home learning environment, family background, reading skills/habits, career goals/mindset, and mental health), the authors train multiple classifiers in RapidMiner. Across models, Gradient Boosted Trees performed best with Accuracy 73.7%, Precision 72.8%, and Recall 88.3%, while Decision Tree performed worst on accuracy/recall. The most important predictors were reading skills/habits (e.g., number of books at home) followed by home learning environment and career goals/mindset. Limitations include the lack of reported complex-survey weighting and limited transparency on the exact PV-combination and imputation rules used for all modeled variables.
